preamble: The shift from the lateral approximant [l] to the labiodental fricative [v] (or more commonly the labial semivowel [w]) is a widespread form of L-vocalization. In Slavic languages, the historical 'dark L' [ɫ] frequently vocalized into a sound that became phonologically identified with /v/ or /w/, particularly in word-final or preconsonantal positions.

examples.note: Historical dark L (ł) shifted to [w] in all positions.
The letter ł is now phonetically identical to the English 'w'.
Masculine past tense and some adjectives show shift of word-final l to o.
Interaction between vocalized liquids and labial fricatives in terminal clusters.

sources: Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic: An Introduction to Slavic Philology.
